data.title: Weekly Indicators: Consumer Spending Strong - Intermodal Volume Growth Decelerates
data.description:
Purpose

I look at the high frequency weekly indicators because while they can be very noisy, they provide a good nowcast of the economy, and will telegraph the maintenance or change in the economy well before monthly or quarterly data is available. They are also an excellent way to "mark your beliefs to market." In general, I go in order of long leading indicators, then short leading indicators, then coincident indicators.

A Note on Methodology

Data is presented in a "just the facts, ma'am" format with a minimum of commentary so that bias is minimized.

Where relevant, I include 12-month highs and lows in the data in parentheses to the right. All data taken from St. Louis FRED unless otherwise linked.

A few items (e.g., Financial Conditions indexes, regional Fed indexes, stock prices, the yield curve) have their own metrics based on long-term studies of their behavior.

Where data is seasonally adjusted, generally it is scored positively if it is within the top 1/3 of that range, negative in the bottom 1/3, and neutral in between. Where it is not seasonally adjusted, and there are seasonal issues, waiting for the YoY change to change sign will lag the turning point. Thus, I make use of a convention: data is scored neutral if it is less than 1/2 as positive/negative as at its 12-month extreme.

With long leading indicators, which by definition turn at least 12 months before a turning point in the economy as a whole, there is an additional rule: data is automatically negative if, during an expansion, it has not made a new peak in the past year, with the sole exception that it is scored neutral if it is moving in the right direction and is close to making a new high.

For all series where a graph is available, I have provided a link to where the relevant graph can be found.

Recap of Monthly Reports

April data included a small increase in consumer prices, but a significant decline in producer prices. Nominal increase in retail sales rose slightly, but declined in real terms. Industrial production was unchanged, but manufacturing production declined. Housing starts increased slightly, but both total and single family permits declined significantly. Consumer sentiment as to both the present and future conditions as initially measured by the U. Of Michigan declined further to near 20-year lows.

Long leading indicators

Interest rates and credit spreads

Rates

  • BAA corporate bond index 6.28%, up +0.02% w/w (1-yr range: 5.33-6.38)
  • 10-year Treasury bonds 4.44%, up +0.05% w/w (3.66-4.98)
  • Credit spread 1.84%, down -0.03% w/w (1.36-2.02).

(Graph at Moody's Seasoned Baa Corporate Bond Yield | FRED | St. Louis Fed.)

Yield curve

  • 10 year minus 2 year: +0.45%, up +0.06% w/w (-1.07 - +0.53)
  • 10 year minus 3 month: +0.09%, up +0.01% w/w (-1.65 - 0.17)
  • 2 year minus Fed funds: -0.34%, up +0.10% w/w.

(Graph at 10-Year Treasury Constant Maturity Minus 2-Year Treasury Constant Maturity | FRED | St. Louis Fed.)

30-Year conventional mortgage rate (from Mortgage News Daily)

  • 6.92%, up +0.03% w/w (6.11-7.52).

Treasury and corporate bond yields are well below their 12-month highs of several months ago, as are mortgage rates. Nevertheless they remain a negative. The spreads between Treasuries and investment grade and high-yield bonds, which blew out in early April, have returned to their previous range.

Housing

Mortgage applications (from the Mortgage Bankers Association)

  • Purchase apps up +2% to 154 (114-163) [SA]
  • Purchase apps 4 wk avg. up +1 to 145 [SA]
  • Purchase apps YoY% up +18% [NSA]
  • Purchase apps YoY 4 wk avg. +10% [NSA]
  • Refi apps down -0.4% w/w [SA]
  • Refi apps YoY up +44% [SA].

*[SA] = seasonally adjusted, [NSA] = not seasonally adjusted.

Real Estate Loans (from the FRB)

  • Down less than -0.1% w/w
  • Up +1.2% YoY.

(Graph at Real Estate Loans, All Commercial Banks | FRED | St. Louis Fed.)

Both purchase mortgage and refinancing applications have been volatile, but within a limited range over the past 18 months. Both are now higher YoY, but are close to their longer-term bottoms, so they score neutral.

In 2023, real estate loans sank below 1/2 of their 12-month-high, the last housing indicator to turn negative, and have generally continued to worsen.

Money supply

The Federal Reserve has discontinued this weekly series. Data is now only released monthly. March data was just released three weeks ago:

  • M1 m/m up +0.2%, YoY Real M1 up +0.8% (3 year high) (worst: -1.4% 8/23)
  • M2 m/m up +0.4%, YoY Real M2 up +1.7% (3 year high) (worst -8.6% 9/23).

No recession has happened without a YoY real M1 negative, or YoY real M2 below +2.5%. Real M2 fell below that threshold in March 2022. Real M1 also turned negative as of May 2022. In the past, when real money supply has rebounded to the levels it is at now, it is either during the latter part of a recession, or in the period of recovery, so both of these are positive long leading metrics now.

Corporate profits (Q1 90% actual + 10% estimated) from I/B/E/S via FactSet) at p. 33

  • Q1 up +0.12 to 64.01, down -2.7% q/q.

FactSet estimates earnings, which are replaced by actual earnings as they are reported, and are updated weekly. The "neutral" band is +/-3%. I also average the previous two quarters together, until at least 100 companies have actually reported. That briefly happened early this quarter, which means this metric turned negative. But as earnings have come in, it has improved sharply from its original -8% range all the way to neutral.

Credit conditions (from the Chicago Fed)

  • Financial Conditions Index down -0.07 (looser) to -0.51 (-0.42 - -0.67)
  • Adjusted Index (removing background economic conditions) down -0.06 (looser) to -0.57(-0.50 - -0.69)
  • Leverage subindex down -0.16 (looser) to -0.37 (+0.13 - -0.76).

In these indexes, lower = better for the economy. The Chicago Fed's Adjusted Index's real break-even point is roughly -0.25. In the leverage index, a negative number is good, a positive poor. The historical breakeven point has been -0.5 for the unadjusted Index. Recently, this index went below -0.60, warranting a rating change to positive, but they have since retreated back into the neutral range. Despite its recent big upward move, the leverage subindex was still positive for the economy; and it has also improved in the past few weeks.

Short leading indicators

Economic Indicators from the late Jeff Miller’s “Weighing the Week Ahead”

  • Miller Score (formerly "C-Score"): up +34 to 421, up +31 m/m (209 5/24/24 - 421 5/16/25) Components: Yield curve 50 Phillips curve 371.
  • St. Louis Fed Financial Stress Index: down -0.1326 to -0.4793 (+0.0944 4/18/25 - -.9762 1/31/25).
  • BCIp from Georg Vrba: up +10.3 to +49.0 as of 5/1/25 iM's Business Cycle Index (100 is max value, below 25 is recession signal averaging 20 weeks ahead).

The Miller Score is designed to look 52 weeks ahead for whether or not a recession is possible. Any score over 500 means no recession. This number fell below that threshold at the beginning of August 2021, so not only is it negative, but we are now years into the “recession eligible” time period. This indicator is heavily influenced by both the yield curve and the still very low unemployment rate vs. gas prices(expecting a reversal) which explains why it remains negative. My colleague who compiles this score began disaggregating the two components several weeks ago.

The St. Louis Financial Stress index is one where a negative score is a positive for the economy, and during its limited existence, has risen above zero before a recession by less than one year. It remained very positive until briefly in April. It has now recovered.

Since last April IM has stated that their system does not forecast a recession.

Trade weighted US$

  • Up +0.02 to 122.88 w/w, up +0.4% YoY (last week) (broad) (118.76 - 129.67) (Graph at Nominal Broad U.S. Dollar Index
  • Up +0.71 to 101.08 w/w, down -3.2% YoY (major currencies) (graph at link) (99.01- 109.66).

The neutral range for the US$ is between unchanged to 5% higher YoY. At 5% higher or more YoY, the strength suppresses exports and incentivizes imports, a negative for the economy. The trend in these measures had been from below 0 (thus positive) to neutral, to above 5% (negative) for 18 months, until the last couple of months. The broad US$ retreated under that threshold five weeks ago, and remains neutral, while the major currencies index declined to significantly lower YoY, so a positive.

Commodity prices

Bloomberg Commodity Index

  • Down -1.80 to 100.96 (93.30 - 107.71)
  • Down -4.5% YoY.

(Graph at https://www.marketwatch.com/investing/index/bcom?countrycode=xx.)

Bloomberg Industrial metals ETF (from Bloomberg) (graph at link)

  • 142.00, up +.0.35 w/w (130.47 -173.08)
  • Down -12.2% YoY.

Both of these measures improved earlier last year and industrial commodities surged to new 12 month highs in the autumn before backing off. The broad measure then turned back negative, and has since oscillated between that and neutral. Two months ago, the industrial measure weakened to neutral for one week before rebounding, and then fell all the way to negative. Five weeks ago, the broad commodity index declined sharply into negative territory, but then rebounded into neutral range. By contrast, the industrial metals measure remains very negative.

Stock prices S&P 500 (from CNBC) (graph at link)

  • Up +5.6% to 5974.75.

There was an all-time high twelve weeks ago. Since the metric is based on making three month new highs and lows, if there is no new high on Monday, this indicator will turn negative due to the big low in April.

Regional Fed New Order Indexes

(*indicates report this week)

  • *Empire State up +15.8 to +7.0
  • *Philly up 36.9 to 7.5
  • Richmond down -9 to -13
  • Kansas City up +1 to -11
  • *Dallas down -19.9 to -20.0
  • Month-over-month rolling average: down -5 to -18.

The regional average is more volatile than the ISM manufacturing index, but usually correctly forecasts its month-over-month direction. Since spring 2022, these gradually declined to neutral and then negative. The indexes varied sharply last year, but recently became more variable, first turning neutral and then one month+ ago to positive. This may have reflected manufacturers’ attempting to front-run new tariffs - which would account to the sharp reversals beginning in March.

Last month I wrote that “I am strongly considering adding the regional service indexes this month, to see if the consumer segment of the economy suddenly shrinks in as close to real time as reasonably available.” Those reports are posted below, with new reports posted in the past week noted with an *:

  • *Empire State up 3.6 to -16.2
  • Philly up +1.3 to +6.9
  • Richmond down -16 to -30
  • Kansas City up +3 to +3
  • Dallas down -8.1 to -19.4
  • Average: -12.

In contrast to the above non-manufacturing reports, the ISM non-manufacturing report turned more positive in April.

Employment metrics

Initial jobless claims

  • 229,000, unchanged w/w, up +3.2% YoY
  • 4-week average 230,500, up +2,250 w/w, up +6.1% YoY.

(Graph at St. Louis FRED.)

Jobless claims were positive for most of 2024, except for hurricane-influenced weeks during the autumn. They subsequently returned to neutral, then briefly positive, before turning back to neutral. Should the four-week average climb above 10% higher YoY, that would warrant a change of rating to negative, but there is no indication as of now of any imminent changes.

Temporary staffing index (from the American Staffing Association) (graph at link)

  • Unchanged at 84 w/w
  • Down -5.9% YoY (low -13.7%- high -5.9%).

This series has been negative since early 2023. YoY comparisons continued to be negative throughout 2024. It remains frankly recessionary. I suspect this has been a secular change and giving a false signal as a result. Relatively speaking, the YoY reading has been trending “less bad” for the past number of months, and finally, since it is now more than half the way to unchanged on a YoY basis, to neutral as of several weeks ago.

Tax Withholding (from the Department of the Treasury)

  • $253.4 B for the last 20 reporting days vs. $234.5 B last year, up +$18.9 B or +8.1%.

These were very positive for almost all of 2024, and the trend continued this year with the exception of three weeks - until April 15, which may have been affected by a change in the way Treasury recorded electronic payments that day. That glitch went out of the comparison period this week, and as suspected, collections have returned to being very positive.

Oil prices and usage (from the E.I.A.)

  • Oil up +$1.50 to $62.39 w/w, down -15.4% YoY ($58.21 - $83.88)
  • Gas prices down -$0.03 to $3.12 w/w, down -$0.49 YoY
  • Usage 4-week average up +3.8% YoY.

(Information can be found at “This Week In Petroleum” at the E.I.A.’s website.)

Oil prices first declined into the bottom 1/3rd of their 3-year range, and so turned positive several months ago. With the crash in commodity prices several weeks ago, they are now very positive. The reason I score oil prices this way, as opposed to other commodities, is their huge effect on the consumer wallet.

Gas prices also recently made new 3-year lows, and so are also positive. Last year, mileage driven had been generally negative. It turned neutral and then positive earlier this year, but in March, it retreated to neutral for several weeks before turning negative in April. Two weeks ago, it turned back positive.

Note: Given this measure’s extreme volatility, I believe the best measure is against their 3-year average.

Bank lending rates

  • 4.31% Secured Overnight Financing Rate [SOFR], up +0.02% w/w.

The TED Spread has been discontinued, and as of last September, so was LIBOR. At the suggestion of a reader, I began to track the SOFR instead. Unfortunately, SOFR has only been in existence since 2018, so there is no track record has to show it might behave around normal recessions (vs. the pandemic). Over the past 5 years, it does appear to have matched the trend in LIBOR. Given the alternatives, I am now including it in my list of indicators in the conclusion.

Coincident indicators

St. Louis FRED Weekly Economic Index

  • Down -0.41 to 2.09 w/w (Low 1.65 November 2, 2024 - high 3.21 November 23, 2024).

This measure was in the neutral range during most of early 2024, but generally positive (above 2.00) since then, and - surprisingly - came very close to setting a new 12-month high five weeks ago before backing off.

Restaurant reservations YoY (from Open Table) State of the Restaurant Industry | OpenTable

  • 7-day average up +7% YoY, vs. +6% one week ago.

Since 2023, this index has been very volatile, swinging between positive, neutral, and negative several times. In general, this year the YoY trends have improved, but with a great deal of noise.

Consumer spending

  • Johnson Redbook up +5.8% YoY, 4-week average +6.5% (high 7.4% 4/25/25; low 4.8% February 1, 2025) United States Redbook Index.

The Redbook index has been positive since February last year. It has had very good readings for the past six weeks. Its continued strong performance is probably consumers front-running anticipated tariff price increases and shortages.

Consumer inflation by Truflation (Independent, economic & financial data in real time on-chain | Truflation)

  • Up +0.23% to +1.85% YoY (High 3.11% 12/11/24 - Low 1.06% 9/13/24).

This recent addition is a daily update to inflation, similar to the “billion prices project” of the last decade (which required a subscription). I have not added this to my list below of coincident or leading indicators, but needless to say it is an up-to-the-moment reading on this very important indicator. This series has been volatile, and the YoY comparisons had been turning higher since September, but has been very low in the last several months, although it did make a new two-month high this week.

Real Consumer Spending

  • Up +4.0% YoY (high - 6.0% 4/25/25; 12 month low 1.7% 12/16/24).

This metric premiered at the beginning of last year. One of my most important mantras is that consumption leads employment. Real retail sales have a long history of doing so, but are only reported on a monthly basis.

The weekly result is derived simply by subtracting YoY inflation as measured by Truflation from the YoY change in nominal consumer spending as measured by Redbook. While it will be somewhat noisy, it should anticipate changes in the monthly measures ahead of time. It backed off from the 12-month high it set in February, and then gradually rebounded, and made another 12 month high several months ago. After mainly weeks in the upper part of its 12-month range, it was tepid at the beginning of the year, before improving in the past two months, even making a new 12-month high before backing off.

Transport

Railroads (from the AAR)

  • Carloads up +6.2% YoY (cumulative 2025 vs. 2024 up +2.0)
  • Intermodal units up +5.4% YoY (cumulative up +7.9%)
  • Total loads up +5.7% YoY (cumulative up +5.2%).

(Graph at Railfax Report - North American Rail Freight Traffic Carloading Report.)

Shipping transport

  • Harpex unchanged at 2055
  • Baltic Dry Index down -11 to 1305.

For many weeks rail was neutral, and it has continued to be noisy week to week, and mixed for many weeks before turning positive two months ago. The big increases in the past month may reflect businesses attempting to get inventory before the new tariffs kick in. Per port sources, docking at West Coast ports have already declined, but there has been no perceptible decline in the YoY weekly comparisons so far. There is some deceleration in intermodal loads, which were up a cumulative 8.7% over 2024 five weeks ago, a number which has declined every week but one since. Since this may be our earliest warning, I will continue to track this for at least a while.

Harpex was as high as nearly 4500 almost 2 years ago, but declined as low as 810 in December of last year before rising almost relentlessly since, setting repeated 12-month highs in the past three months. The usual interpretation for this is that demand is straining against supply, showing a strong global economy, and thus very positive.

Similarly, the BDI was in a generally declining trend throughout 2022 before bottoming at a three-year low of 530 in February 2023. The overall trend has been higher since then, rising to a high of 3346 last December, probably reflecting the Houthi attacks on shipping in the Red Sea. This year its low was 1309 in January, and its recent high was 2419 last March. It declined to the bottom 1/3rd of its one-year range during the rest of the year, but rebounded in the past nine weeks back into its middle range, and remained in that range this week.

I am wary of reading too much into price indexes like this, since they are heavily influenced by supply (as in, a huge overbuilding of ships in the last decade) as well as demand.

Steel production (American Iron and Steel Institute)

  • Up +0.9% w/w
  • Up +1.8% YoY.

This metric oscillated between positive and negative for most of 2024. In the past several months it was negative before rebounding to positive for three weeks. It then faded to neutral and back to negative for in the past several months, but in the past several weeks it has improved to neutral and now positive for two weeks.

Summary And Conclusion

Below are this week’s spreadsheets of the long leading, short leading, and coincident readings. Check marks indicate the present reading. If there has been a change this week, the prior reading is marked with an X:

Long leading Indicators Positive Neutral Negative
Corporate bonds ✓
10 year Treasury ✓
10 yr-2 yr Treasury ✓
10 yr-3 mo. Treasury ✓
2 yr-Fed funds ✓
Mortgage rates ✓
Purchase Mtg. Apps. ✓
Refi Mtg Apps. ✓
Real Estate Loans ✓
Real M1 ✓
Real M2 ✓
Corporate Profits ✓
Adj. Fin. Conditions Index ✓
Leverage Index ✓
Totals: 5 4 5
Short Leading Indicators Positive Neutral Negative
Credit Spread ✓
Miller Score ✓
St. L. Fin. Stress Index ✓
US$ Broad ✓
US$ Major currencies ✓
Total commodities X ✓
Industrial commodities ✓
Stock prices ✓
Regional Fed New Orders ✓
Initial jobless claims ✓
Temporary staffing ✓
Gas prices ✓
Oil prices ✓
Gas Usage ✓
Totals: 5 5 4
Coincident Indicators Positive Neutral Negative
Weekly Econ. Index ✓
Open Table ✓
Redbook ✓
Rail ✓
Harpex ✓
BDI ✓
Steel ✓
Tax Withholding ✓ X
TED & LIBOR (deleted)
SOFR ✓
Financial Cond. Index ✓
Totals: 8 2 0

.

There were few changes this week.

The long leading indicators remain neutral. Q1 corporate profits, which started out estimated to be very negative, have now improved to neutral.

The short leading indicators were negative for three weeks before returning to positive two weeks ago. They softened to neutral this week, as commodities declined YoY.

The coincident indicators are very positive, as consumer spending continues to hold up nicely. As anticipated, tax withholding returned to being positive with the April 15 glitch out of the comparison. So far, rail traffic shows little evidence that the trans-Pacific decline in shipping due to the trade war is having an effect, except that YoY comparisons of intermodal containers while positive are decelerating.

So long as jobless claims do not turn negative, and consumer spending continues to power forward, there appears to be little imminent risk to the economy for the moment.

data.title: Pollard Banknote: iGaming Punch Seems To Be Priced In
data.description:
In my last write-up on Pollard Banknote (TSX:PBL:CA)—that century-old legend from Winnipeg known for printing scratch-offs, powering bingo nights, and now diving into the iLottery world—I said the stock was flirting with a pretty attractive entry point, somewhere in the $18s.

Around that time, Pollard had just dropped its Q4 FY 2024 results—and to be fair, the numbers looked solid, especially on the bottom-line, thanks to their high-margin iLottery business doing the heavy lifting.

But despite the strong showing, the stock had been sliding, down nearly 20% YTD.

Why? Well, the market was having a full-blown flashback to the 1970s: inflation was supposedly staging a comeback, partly thanks to the so-called ‘Trump Tariffs,’ and folks started panicking about a pullback in discretionary spending.

Basically, everyone was bracing for stagflation.

From March to mid-April, the stock kept sliding, eventually reaching $18 on April 21st. That just so happens to be right in the sweet spot I mentioned in my first write-up—an entry point I still consider pretty attractive.

At that level, you'd be getting a margin of safety of at least 28%, with a potential upside of around 39% to $25. Throw in the modest 1% dividend yield, and honestly, that’s not a bad setup at all.

After reaching this bottom, the stock encountered buying pressure that took it above the $20s before turning back down again after the release of the Q1 FY 2025 results.

Revisiting my first rating, the 'Hold' rating seems to have aged well.

Today, the stock is trading at nearly 1% below my first write-up, being outperformed by both peers, such as Accel Entertainment (ACEL), and the S&P, which is up over 7.5% since April 2.

But I’ve still got a couple of things on my mind. And I hope we’ll heal together before the end of this article:

  1. Will we really see it dip below $18? And even if we do, would it really matter all that much if you grabbed it at $18 or somewhere around $19 or $20? I mean, up to the $25s, we would have a potential upside of 31% to 25%, with a margin of safety in the double digits as well. In this situation, I intend to compare Pollard's total return if we see it reach these levels by FY 2025, as well as compare it to benchmarks.
  2. The sensitivity of the valuation to the gross margin that Pollard will deliver going forward. This seems like a minor detail at this point, but perhaps the biggest driver for the bear thesis is the impact of iGaming on iLottery's operations and how this could compress margins.

To start digging into those questions, we’ve got to take a closer look at Q1 FY 2025. The stock had just come off a decent rally after hitting its low, but right after the earnings dropped, it slipped back into a downtrend and fell below the $20 mark again.

So, what did Q1 really tell us?

I can tell you that business is healthy, as we can see from the following data:

  1. Revenues reached $142.3 million in Q1 FY 2025, growing approximately 16.3% year-over-year. When we consolidate revenues from NeoPollard (Pollard’s joint venture and a key driver of iLottery), we have a total of $177 million, growing 17% year-over-year due to the momentum of the branch business.
  2. Pollard completed its efforts to provide an iLottery solution in Kansas on February 13. This was the fastest launch ever in North America for iLottery. eInstants have also shown strong performance in the Virginia and North Carolina Lottery.
  3. In the Lottery segment, Pollard rolled out broad-based price increases wherever the market allowed. This helped lift the average spend per player and gave margins a nice boost in the process.
  4. Higher volumes of charitable gaming boosted sales by $4.4 million in Q1 2025.
  5. Pollard also completed its acquisition of Pacific Gaming, which expands its presence in the charitable gaming space—specifically by adding portable electronic devices for bingo and other charitable games to its portfolio.
  6. 84% year-on-year growth in EBIT and around 70% year-on-year growth in net income.

You see, these were solid results—no doubt about that.

But a few details ended up stealing the spotlight and giving the market a bit of a bittersweet aftertaste.

Let’s start with the repricing of instant tickets.

Honestly, this was the lesser of evils. But why do I say this? Well, the impact of repricing alone increased sales by at least $17.1 million year-over-year, with a reduction of approximately $5.8 million in instant ticket sales volume. This created a net positive effect of at least $11.3 million.

The other thing to note is that, even though Charity Games revenue went up by $4.4 million, eTab revenue dropped by $1.7 million.

Last but not least, the weak performance of the Michigan iLottery—dragged down by direct competition from iGaming—was probably the biggest headache of the quarter. It could turn into Pollard Banknote’s worst nightmare going forward.

Just as iLottery keeps gaining traction, iGaming isn’t sitting still either.

It’s being legalized in more and more states every year, riding a wave of liberalization, especially as states try to plug budget holes without as much help from federal transfers.

Take Michigan, for example. It’s basically a case study. After the Lawful Internet Gaming Act and the Lawful Sports Betting Act kicked in back in late 2019, iLottery—despite being around since 2014 through NeoPollard—started losing steam.

Basically, the same logic that makes iLottery so appealing to states is also what’s fueling the spread of iGaming. The catch? iLottery just doesn’t have the firepower to compete head-to-head with the iGaming giants flooding the space.

Maybe the secret lies in iLottery’s migration to iGaming, but that’s just my impression.

To give you an idea of the scale: iGaming alone brought in $260.5 million in gross revenue in March, up 26.5% year-over-year.

If you look at it in absolute terms, the hit was fairly mild. Pollard saw $1.5 million in year-over-year headwinds.

But because iLottery is a high-margin segment, Pollard saw a gross profit of $25.4 million—or a gross margin of 17.4%. This is about 10bps higher year-over-year, but down from the 21% they achieved in Q2 FY 2024.

But don’t get me wrong, margins weren’t squeezed just because of headwinds.

One of the big factors was actually a strategic move: Pollard also saw higher upfront costs for iLottery’s entry into Kansas—a state that has yet to legalize iGaming, only sports betting in 2022.

On the bottom line, here’s what really stood out:

  1. EBITDA of $13.7 million, growing 15% year-over-year, and with a margin of 9.36%. This is higher than Pollard's 5-year average of 7.6%, but still lags behind most of FY 2024.
  2. EBIT of $2.1 million, growing 75% year-over-year and maintaining a margin of 1.47%. Again, this is an improvement over the 0.92% EBIT margin of Q1 FY 2024, but it is a reduction from the 4.95% and 6% we saw in the following two quarters.
  3. Net income of $11.7 million, driven by $16.2 million from the NeoPollard stake. This represents nearly 70% year-over-year growth in net income and a margin of 8.02% (6.6% when considering revenues consolidated with those of NeoPollard).
  4. Diluted EPS of $0.43, significantly higher than the $0.25 in Q1 FY 2024 and comparable to the $0.44 reported in Q2 FY 2024.
  5. Negative OCF of $25.7 million due to pressures from receivables. Since Capex in the period was $8.9 million and the acquisition cost $6.9 million, Pollard saw a compressed FCF of -$41.5 million. The outflow was debt, with Pollard making a net issuance of $46.3 million, paying dividends and other financing activities, and still having a cash surplus of $3.8 million.
  6. Long-term debt came in at $198.6 million (Debt/EBITDA ratio of just 1.96x if we adjust for NeoPollard's stake of over $50 million in the TTM), nothing to worry about here.

Very fair results to me, to be honest.

But that still doesn't tell us what the impact of a more affected gross margin would be for an iLottery challenged by iGaming? I'll explore that in the valuation.

Two Questions, One Answer

Now’s the moment of truth. I’m going to try to gauge the impact of these tighter margins and figure out if that really justifies pulling the trigger at $19-something instead of waiting for $18 flat.

The consensus EPS estimate for FY 2025 is $1.96. Note that this implies a net income of approximately $53 million, representing a net profit margin of around 8.75%. To give you an idea, Pollard's 5-year average net profit margin is 5.3%, representing an improvement of at least 200bps year-over-year and 345bps over the 5-year average.

Let's call the analysts' projection the base case.

Here, we would have a projected P/E of 9.94x, which is more than 50% lower than the 5-year average that Pollard used to trade. Adjusting for EPS growth through PEG, we would have at least 50% year-over-year growth, which would give us an adjusted multiple of 0.2. Not bad at all.

But let's take a look at the VGTs, iGaming, and similar segments, what do the multiples tell us?

  • International Game Technology (IGT) trades at a P/E of 19.1x
  • Light & Wonder (LNW) trades at a P/E of 14.9x (PEG of 0.4)
  • Inspired Entertainment (INSE) trades at a P/E of 12.3x
  • Accel trades at a P/E of 13.3x

The average P/E gives us a multiple of 14.9x. If we exclude IGT from the comparison group, we would have a multiple of 13.5x. With the estimated EPS for FY 2025, the base case would give us a price target of $29.20 to $26.50.

In short, this is similar to our initial target of $25 based on NPVGO.

But what if we see weakness on the margins, would there still be such a strong upside?

Let's assume the same projected revenue of $605.18 million. With a net margin of 6% to 7% (a range that already assumes a sequential improvement from the 5-year average but still close to where we've been recently), we'd be looking at a net income of $36.31 million to $42.36 million.

That would be an EPS of around $1.34 to $1.56—a difference that's at least 20% lower than analysts' estimates.

Using the EPS of $1.45 (the midpoint of the bear case estimates), we could say that Pollard shares would be trading at a P/E of 13.44x.

Look at the interesting discovery we made here: if Pollard continues to be pressured by iGaming, the shares would be trading slightly below the average of its peers.

This means, in practice, that at $19.50, the stock is slightly undervalued if the business continues to be pressured. If we consider the control group's midpoint P/E (14.2x), we would be looking at a price target of $20.60 in the worst-case scenario.

If you ask me why P/E is one of the best ways to look at Pollard's valuation, the answer is simple. Only through it can we see NeoPollard's impact on the results. We can't do that with EBITDA, much less with EBIT.

Both scenarios give us the following outlook for Pollard:

  • Expected scenario: price target is $25, but could reach up to $29. At $19.50, where we are, we would have a potential upside of up to 48.7% if the stock reaches the upper end of the estimates. I prefer to stay with the upside of 28.2%, which would still give us a margin of safety of 22%. Considering the dividend yield of 1.02%, the total return of the stock could be almost 30% if the price reaches my price target by the end of the year.
  • Bear scenario: with the price target for this scenario at $20.60, the potential upside would be 5.64%, with a margin of safety of 5.3%. The total return here would be somewhere around 7%.

If we stack this up against a basic benchmark, we start to get a clearer picture of what kind of return we should be aiming for. Take the Canadian 10-year bond, which is yielding about 3.15% annually. Now tack on a risk premium—say, 7.28% for small caps, adjusted to reflect Pollard’s profile (a market cap a bit over $500 million and a beta of 0.91)—and you land on a required return just north of 10%.

Honestly, it seems to me that this bet is worth it.

With a downside limited to the undervaluation itself and a trade-off of approximately 3% annual return (benchmark — bear case), Pollard shares could give us an upside of almost 30% at this point.

I think I’m more than happy to take the almost 2% discount we’ve seen since the last write-up and go ahead and upgrade my rating to a ‘Buy’ here, still holding on to my $25 price target.

And hey, if you got in at $18—congrats. Waiting for the perfect discount is great and all, but it's not worth sitting on the sidelines if it means missing out on some pretty tasty upside.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Booz Allen Hamilton: Policy Risk Is Priced In
data.description:
Booz Allen Hamilton Holding Corporation (BAH) has undergone corrections since the US elections and despite a recent rally from the tariff sell-offs, is still around 33% down from the top. The sell-off from the top around November 2024 onwards, with increased volume, was due to concerns around federal budget cuts. The short interest as on date is still reasonably high at around 5%. Let us evaluate whether BAH's corrections now demand a re-entry or a continuation of what the market is perceiving as a wait and watch mode as the new government's spending policies come together.

Budget Cuts

The Trump administration’s push to reduce federal spending has placed BAH under watch as investors brace for more policy clarity. The DOGE has mandated agencies to re-evaluate their consulting contracts. The uncertainty surrounding contract renewals and budget allocations is hanging over BAH. DOGE has reportedly identified $4b in savings across consulting contracts, impacting Accenture, Deloitte and BAH, among other contractors. The exact specifics of quantified revenue impact on BAH is difficult to ascertain due to the classified and complicated nature of contracts. But even if we assume a $1.5b revenue impact directly from cancellations, which also somewhat ties to the $1b savings offered by BAH in its response proposal, the revenue impact could add up to 10-15%, including risks of further probes and negative outcomes.

Long-Term Tailwinds

Fundamentally, BAH is positioned to benefit from accelerated digital transformation and AI initiatives of U.S. Federal agencies, especially in the defense and civilian sectors, despite potential uncertainties and short-term headwinds. This secular tailwind is likely to play out eventually in the long term and when that happens, BAH, because of its dominant position in federal AI services, will definitely benefit.

The risk of a 98% exposure to government contracts is both a concentration risk, particularly in the current scenario, and also a moat as contracts are sticky and customers are less cyclical. There is less room for budget cuts or customer drops due to an economic slowdown. The recent uncertainty could therefore provide a contrarian entry point into a well established government agency player with significant advantages.

Recent setups of higher cyber and geopolitical threats augur well for BAH's demand pipeline. With a wide capability offering from cybersecurity to analytics to cloud integration, BAH is very well suited to cater to government project needs. The barrier to entry is further solidified by the clearance and waiting requirements that the personnel from BAH have already obtained. Onboarding any new player would require time and operational discipline to replicate, even for larger commercial tech companies. Expertise developed in niche areas such as defense analytics and classified AI projects is another advantage BAH has over new entrants in the space. BAH benefits from its well entrenched experience in areas such as autonomous systems and quantum secure communications.

The capabilities that BAH has developed from its experience may suffer from compliance drags, preventing expansion across geographies and governments or even venturing into enterprises. However, the recent acquisition of its cybersecurity arm SnapAttack, goes to show how its generic capabilities can still be leveraged to unlock value and create optionality for investors.

Finally, BAH's response to the cost-cutting measures highlights how partnering with BAH actually indirectly increases federal agencies' efficiencies through cutting-edge technology adoption and positions BAH as a cost center and cost cutter in itself. The cost savings directly offered by BAH is over the lifetime of the contracts. I believe, as we will see later, that despite a potential revenue impact of 10-15%, conservatively assuming it is an immediate and annual cut, the sticky positioning, generalized use cases of acquired skill sets and future modernization and mission-critical defense upgrades of the agencies will make a strong bullish case for BAH. A contrarian position now also benefits from the fact that the DOGE recommendations are not yet finalized and may likely go through several layers of political pushback to legal scrutiny. Betting on policy softening (as is being seen in the US China tariffs), particularly when the policy risk premium is likely built in, makes for an apt contrarian entry in a fundamentally long-term bet.

Financials Strong but Need Efficiencies

Assuming a $1b impact and looking at overall revenues and gross margins, it becomes important to monitor management commentary and actions around efficiencies. As such, a 50% gross margin level is good for a consulting firm and should be able to absorb margin shocks of the $1b quantum at stake. Margins could shrink, but the exact impact can be cushioned by working around fixed costs, repurposing resources, and driving efficiencies around R&D and administration costs.

BAH’s ~$500 million in cash and cash equivalents provides a reasonable short-term liquidity buffer to absorb a revenue shock, but is unlikely to fully offset a $1 billion decline in revenue. With a 50% gross margin, the revenue drop translates to about a $500 million gross profit loss, which would significantly pressure reserves.

The company’s current free cash flow of ~$133 million indicates solid cash generation, but this could quickly shrink or turn negative if costs aren’t cut in response to falling revenue. Ultimately, BAH’s financial resilience will depend on how effectively it manages costs and introduces efficiencies as and when the revenue drags start showing up.

Investors can take comfort from the fact that there are several layers of assumptions in the scenario analyzed. The $1b impact in itself is a higher end assumption and while it is likely to be spread across the contractual lifetime, I have assumed an immediate impact. BAH will have several recourses to manage this, but a look at the worst case does highlight two things - one, the impact is not terminal, and two, it can be managed if BAH responds with efficiencies.

Valuations the Key for Entry

Given the fact that any impact on topline is significant (if it translates from proposals to implementation, and to the degree currently expected), valuations are the key determinant of a contrarian entry. If the worst case of policy risk is already factored in, we could enter not only on a policy softening expectation, but also on the long-term fundamentals as discussed earlier.

Here is a potential impact of a $1b revenue hit on EPS calculations assuming minimal operational efficiencies. A 33% drop in EPS in a relatively stressful case (if not worst), implies a 33% price correction from the top is a potentially decent entry point.

Can Initiate Starter Position Before Results

Overall, the risks are moderately high, but much of it appears factored in for BAH. The secular tailwinds for the long term are intact. Significant contrarian starter positions can be initiated in BAH with some fuel left to accumulate on dips. The stickiness of longer-term government contracts and legal and political challenges to budget cut recommendations could cushion the worst-case assumptions.

BAH's Q4 2025 results become important for further accumulation, and investors will possibly need to look beyond numbers. Results may show early signs of pressure from the DOGE proposals, but more significant financial impacts are more likely to show up in Q1 FY 2026 or later. Management guidance and backlog trends will be key indicators of risk. Keep an eye on management commentary turning cautious or more details on BAH's response to the budget cuts, including expansion or efficiencies. I expect efficiencies or repurposing pointers to soothe anxiety around margin pressures. Starter positions now could benefit from that and any potential short squeeze due to the moderately high short interest buildup.

data.title: 2 Underrated Dividend Growers, I'm Eager To Buy On Any Dip
data.description:
Introduction

  • Capital
  • Energy
  • Freedom
  • Always buying the dip

You're probably confused by this very random start to my article.

The first three bullet points were brought up by Richard J. Gonzalez, who wrote an article titled "What Makes America Great?" for the Dallas Chapter Society for the Advancement of Management in 1951.

The article, which was provided by the American Heritage Education Foundation, was, as the title already gave away, about things that make America so great. Access to capital, abundant energy, and personal freedoms were three reasons that explained America's high standard of living.

To this day, these things are still true, as I wrote in an article titled "Red, White And Wealth: Building An All-American Dividend Portfolio."

If I had found the Gonzales article earlier than today, I would have included the following quote in my own article, as I love how well he described how the abundant use of capital helped America to prosper (emphasis added):

It is the continuous creation of new capital which brings about economic progress, not merely the use of the capital existing today. Regardless of ownership, there must be enough incentive for someone to do without something today so that work may be applied to building new machines which will support future productive capacity. That incentive is the price paid for the use of capital in the form of interest and profits, for without that price future capital would not be forthcoming. Actually, the price paid to the creators of capital is a very small one, especially when we consider the additional production made possible by that capital. - Richard J. Gonzalez (1951)

America has always been the land of opportunity, supported by people's willingness to put capital to work. This entrepreneur-friendly sentiment has created Silicon Valley and funded countless companies that now dominate global industries in areas like artificial intelligence, aerospace, healthcare, consumer electronics, and many others.

When combined with a culture of risk-taking, it creates fertile ground for long-term success. It's also one of the reasons why almost every penny of my net worth is invested in the United States of America (emphasis added):

The USA offers a robust financial infrastructure, including venture capital firms, angel investors, and well-developed capital markets. Entrepreneurs in the USA benefit from a diverse range of funding options, enabling them to secure the necessary capital to start and scale their ventures.

[...] The entrepreneurial spirit runs deep in the USA, fostering a culture that celebrates innovation and risk-taking. From legendary entrepreneurs like Steve Jobs and Elon Musk to countless success stories emerging from every corner of the country, the US culture encourages individuals to pursue their dreams and disrupt traditional industries. - ResearchFDI

This brings me to the fourth bullet point, which is buying the dip, something Ben Carlson highlighted as one of the reasons for American exceptionalism.

I found his tweet through an article titled "Buying The Dip Still Works - Even In This New World," which brought something up that we have discussed as well in the past few weeks: the risk appetite of retail investors.

Unlike hedge funds, which were net sellers, retail investors pushed more capital into the markets. By the end of 2024, individual investors held $35 trillion worth of U.S. stocks, accounting for 38% of the entire market(!). They also entered this year, responsible for nearly a fifth of the total trading volume.

According to the Bloomberg article, at Charles Schwab, which has 37 million active accounts, customers executed roughly 10 million trades per day in the first two weeks of April, 36% more on a year-over-year basis.

However, instead of blindly trading the market in panic mode, they were net buyers of $40 billion of U.S. stocks, which marks a new monthly inflow record.

After the pandemic, the 2022 bear market, and the trade war, dip buying has, once again, proven to be the winning strategy, with the Dot-com strategy being the one rare exception that made buying the dip extremely hard for people.

The dot-com crash stands as the rare exception to what has otherwise been a remarkably successful strategy. But therein lies the risk: With each successful dip bought, more capital and conviction flow into the strategy. The very reliability of the pattern may be quietly building its own vulnerability. - Bloomberg

Interestingly enough, even rapid recovery usually led to more gains. In the 24 trading days after bottoming, the S&P 500 returned 18%. Since 1950, this has happened only six times, excluding the current rally. In five of these scenarios, the market rose by double digits in the 12 months that followed.

Personally, I have deployed a lot of cash in April. As most of my readers are aware, I had no idea if the market was about to bottom. All I knew was that I liked the risk/reward.

Sure, stocks can always drop further. But so what?

Stocks can always drop.

During strong bull markets, we face risks. However, we often choose to either ignore them or misjudge them due to market sentiment. When stocks are in a bear market, we tend to be scared of further losses.

Although every single situation is different, and we will continue to discuss new market declines once they occur, I am a firm believer in ignoring one's feelings when markets drop. If the companies we like are trading at a rare discount, history shows that pulling the trigger is often the right decision.

Having said all of this, in the second part of this article, I'll present two dividend (growth) stocks that often fly under the radar. Two stocks that deserve a lot more attention because I believe they bring something very special to the table.

While they are not your 'average' dividend stocks, I am watching all of them for potential investments in the weeks and months ahead.

Now, let's dive into them!

But first, a quick note:

A Quick Note

Whenever I mention the word 'dividend' without providing at least one idea with a yield of 4% or more, some readers tend to get upset. To some degree, I get that.

So, please note that this article is focused on somewhat unusual dividend growers. In addition to having a focus on dividend growth instead of income, they all have very unusual (and fascinating) business models, which is what this article is mainly about.

Franco-Nevada Corporation (FNV) - A Superior Gold Play

I have been bullish on gold for a while.

This includes Franco-Nevada, which is a company often classified as a miner.

My most recent in-depth article on the company was written on March 24, 2024. Since then, shares have returned 37%, beating the S&P 500's 13% return by a decent margin.

Now, gold prices are dipping again, after President Trump's deal with China has substantially lowered the risks of an escalating trade war.

While I have no idea where gold will bottom, I believe it's a good investment on weakness, as major issues like government deficit problems have not been resolved. If anything, longer-term bond rates continue to surge, making the job of Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent extremely hard.

However, instead of buying gold, I like to buy equities, as I always prefer to own cash flow-generating companies over direct commodity exposure.

That's where Franco-Nevada comes in: a company that is, in fact, NOT a miner.

The Canadian Franco-Nevada Corporation operates as a royalty and streaming company within the natural resources sector, with a focus on precious metals and energy assets.

As we can see below, 79% of its revenues come from precious metals, with gold being the overwhelming contributor to these revenues.

Unlike traditional mining or energy companies, Franco-Nevada does not engage in direct mining operations or exploration. Instead, it provides upfront capital to mining and energy companies in exchange for long-term royalty interests or metal streaming agreements.

To put it differently, mining companies in need of capital for various projects avoid taking on debt or diluting shareholders by working with Franco-Nevada. In exchange for capital, they allow Franco Nevada to buy a fixed amount of product (i.e., gold or silver) below the spot price.

This allows Franco Nevada to benefit from rising commodity prices without taking on any direct operational costs and risks. As we can see below, this has led to an 86% adjusted EBITDA margin last year. It had a net income margin of 56%, one of the best numbers across the entire stock market.

Moreover, because of this model, the longer the life of a mine, the higher the margins, as Franco-Nevada has some major investments that have long been paid off. Candelaria, for example, required an investment of more than $600 million. So far, the company has received way more than that, pushing the return on investment beyond 100%.

As we can see below, it generates royalties and streams from 119 producing mines across the entire globe. It has 430 total projects, which means many of these deals will start to generate income for Franco-Nevada in the future.

This also includes Cobre Panama, one of the world's leading copper mines that has been non-operational since the end of 2023. Franco-Nevada owns streaming rights but has not received any benefits since then.

During its 1Q25 earnings call, the company noted that the Panamanian government is willing to negotiate a deal in 2025, with recent statements suggesting that this could be achieved by the end of this year.

To show you how big of a deal this mine is, Franco-Nevada contributed roughly $1.4 billion to its construction. It accounted for 18% of its revenue in 2021. If this mine becomes operational again, FNV is in for a huge boost, especially in light of the strong surge in commodity prices since then.

In general, the company is in a great spot to boost output, as it could see a surge in GEOs (gold equivalent ounces) from 463K in 2024 to up to 550K by 2029. Including Cobre Panama, that number could rise by another 150 thousand ounces. That's more than 40% potential growth(!).

With that said, in an environment of very high precious metal prices, the demand for financing is lower. Franco-Nevada is more likely to sign new deals when prices are low. That's when miners require financing.

However, that's okay, as the company has enough projects to fully participate in what could be a prolonged uptrend in commodity prices.

It also has a great dividend track record.

Franco-Nevada, which pays its dividend in American dollars, has hiked its dividend every single year since 2008. While it yields just 1%, the dividend has grown by 13% per year since its initiation, regardless of the price of gold.

While it will take a long time for this 1% to turn into a decent yield on cost, it is a great addition to what I expect to be elevated capital gains in the years and decades ahead.

On top of that, over the past ten years, its superior business model has led to massive outperformance compared to gold and gold miners, meaning its business model does not just sound good, but it also results in alpha.

To me, Franco-Nevada is the best of many worlds, including the safety and longer-term potential of gold, the benefits of its ultra-high margin profile, and 'hidden' potential from its Panama assets.

With regard to its valuation, the company has a multi-decade average P/E ratio of 50.9x.

Yes, really.

That's because of its ultra-high margin profile, as it does not have CapEx requirements. Most of its revenue ends up as free cash flow, as it is not a traditional mine. Because of this, the quality of its earnings is higher, which deserves a higher multiple.

Currently, Franco-Nevada trades at 43.7x earnings. Analysts see a path to $4.89 in EPS by 2027, implying a fair stock price of $214, 36% above its current price. This is based on its current valuation.

In other words, if gold continues to dip, I believe FNV is a terrific dividend grower for a wide range of investors looking to add gold exposure.

Aris Water Solutions (ARIS) - So Good, I May Buy It As Well

As most of my readers will know, 30% of my entire portfolio is invested in Texas Pacific Land (TPL) and LandBridge (LB). I recently updated my investment theses in articles that can be accessed here and here.

Texas Pacific and LandBridge own land in the Permian Basin. In addition to making money from oil and gas royalties, they make money from the water business, which is booming, as the fracking process requires a lot of water.

The average fracking job uses roughly 4 million gallons of water per well – or about as much water as New York City uses every six minutes and about 1.3 percent of the water used by the country's car washes every day. - API

On top of that, fracking produces water.

This brackish water is a byproduct. In some areas, it is perfectly normal for one barrel of oil to come with more than ten barrels of brackish/produced water that needs to be taken care of.

Moreover, while total production growth is declining rapidly, water volumes are rising fast, as the declining quality of oil reserves means more water is needed to drill. The ratio of oil to brackish water also becomes less favorable.

Since 2017, produced water volumes are up more than 350% in the Permian, with the average well now lifting nearly 3.5 barrels of water to surface for every one barrel of oil. According to data from B3 Insight, the Permian is forecast to produce 22.3 million barrels of water a day on average in 2025–enough to fill some 925 million one-gallon jugs every 24 hours. The Delaware is projected to average 13.6 MMbbl/d and the Midland 8.4 MMbbl/d, with 16 MMbbl/d of that total going to disposal and the remainder recycled and reused. - AOGR

Both Texas Pacific and LandBridge benefit from this, as their land is used to inject brackish water back into the ground. Also, their land is home to water pipelines, cleaning facilities, and other operations.

Aris Water Solutions is different. It does not own land. Aris is the company that actually treats water, with a network in some of the best drilling spots of the entire Permian Basin, as we can see below. That area is called the Northern Delaware Basin, a sub-basin of the mighty Permian Basin.

This includes nearly 800 miles of water pipelines, a handling capacity of 1.8 million barrels per day, a recycling capacity of 1.4 million barrels per day, and storage capacity of roughly 16 million barrels.

This business is becoming increasingly relevant for at least two reasons:

  • The water business is booming, as I just explained.
  • Because of rising volumes, it becomes increasingly hard to pump produced water back into the ground, as this can create earthquakes. It will also become increasingly expensive, as the best land is running out of spots to inject more water.

In addition to supplying water for the hydraulic fracturing process, which is highly tied to new completions (oil price sensitive), it works on large-scale desalination of produced water for industrial and agricultural re-use and mineral extraction. It also has some renewable energy projects (solar).

Because of this, adjusted EBITDA reached a new all-time high in 1Q25 despite lower energy prices, which pressured well completions. As we can see, produced water handling volumes soared to 1.2 million barrels per day. When adding roughly 560 thousand barrels handled by its water solutions segment, the total reached 1.75 million daily barrels, a number that has risen by roughly 400 thousand daily barrels over the past two years.

Moreover, the company is also diversifying, buying the McNeill Ranch for $45 million. This deal comes with 45,000 surface acres in Texas and New Mexico, close to the Northern Delaware Basin.

This land is perfect for water development, and it can provide future royalties from water injection, groundwater extraction, caliche sales, and other operations. Essentially, the company is somewhat 'copying' the LandBridge business model this way.

While the current macroeconomic environment is very challenging, the company is not yet seeing lower customer demand, as most of its clients are large operators who do not adjust their production plans on a frequent basis.

However, even if they were to make adjustments, the company is in a good spot, as it has the flexibility to reduce capital expenditures significantly while maintaining stable free cash flow. The company estimates that less than $50 million in annual sustaining capital would be required to maintain flat volumes.

It also helps that the company's operations are in the Delaware Basin's best drilling spots, as the very handy overview below shows.

With regard to its dividend, the company currently pays $0.14 per share per quarter, which translates to a yield of 2.4%.

Earlier this year, the dividend was hiked by 33%. It has a payout ratio of 43%.

In other words, a decent yield comes with aggressive dividend growth potential, which is also reflected in its outlook.

The company, which has a very healthy leverage ratio of 2.2x EBITDA (below its target range of 2.5-3.5x), is expected to grow its EPS by 10% in 2025 and 2026, potentially followed by 17% growth in 2027.

Applying a 20x multiple, we get a fair stock price target of $36, more than 50% above the current price.

Although oil prices remain uncertain, I believe in a longer-term uptrend, causing much higher water demand.

In fact, I like Aris so much that I put it on my watchlist, as I want a piece of this business despite my massive investments in Texas Pacific and LandBridge.

Takeaway

America's greatness has always been based on capital, energy, and freedom.

I'm convinced that's still true today.

The ability to deploy capital, take risks, and innovate fuels long-term prosperity, which is clearly visible on the stock market, as most of us have found out in a very pleasant way so far.

In fact, history shows that staying disciplined during market drops pays off, even if timing the bottom is impossible, and elevated volatility may make us feel very uncomfortable when putting money to work.

When it comes to buying the dip, two companies stand out.

Franco-Nevada isn't a miner but a royalty powerhouse with a gold-backed, high-margin cash machine.

Meanwhile, Aris Water Solutions is a Permian Basin water play so compelling, I might just add it to my portfolio.

Both offer unique value beyond their modest yields because sometimes, the best opportunities aren't the ones with the highest yields.

So, what's the bottom line?

Bet on America, ignore the noise, and let exceptional businesses compound.

Risks To My Thesis

Commodity prices are the biggest risks here. For Franco-Nevada, a prolonged downtrend in gold prices could pressure its income. Aris could suffer from a steep decline in oil prices, as general production in the Delaware Basin would likely suffer.

Moreover, both stocks are prone to regulatory risks, as mine closures and/or restrictions with regard to oil production and water handling could disrupt operations.

However, in both cases, I believe these risks are subdued.

data.title: What Moved Markets This Week
